era_context: The Roman Empire under Trajan (r. 98–117 CE) is at the height of its territorial expansion. Trajan's Parthian War (114–117 CE) is ongoing, with Roman forces advancing into Mesopotamia. The Han Dynasty in China is in its final century of decline. The Parthian Empire is under pressure from Roman expansion. In India, the Satavahana and Kushan empires are at their height. This is a year of major military conflict on the Roman-Parthian frontier.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse documentation)
Mesoamerica and South America (no contemporary written sources)

events.title: Trajan's conquest of Mesopotamia and the Tigris-Euphrates region
events.region: Roman Empire and Parthian Empire (Mesopotamia, Tigris-Euphrates region)
events.description: In 116 CE, Roman forces under Emperor Trajan advance deep into Mesopotamia, conquering the Tigris-Euphrates region and establishing Roman control over former Parthian territories. Trajan's forces capture major cities including Ctesiphon, the Parthian capital. The Roman conquest represents the maximum territorial extent of the Roman Empire. Trajan establishes new provinces in Mesopotamia and the surrounding regions. However, the conquest proves difficult to maintain; Parthian resistance continues, and Roman supply lines are stretched thin. The conquest is celebrated in Rome but proves unsustainable, and Hadrian will abandon these territories upon his accession in 117 CE.
events.certainty_note: Trajan's conquest of Mesopotamia is well-attested in multiple sources. The dates and extent of the conquest are accepted by modern scholarship. Cassius Dio provides a detailed narrative account, and epigraphic and numismatic evidence confirms the conquest.

events.title: Kitos War (Second Jewish Revolt) continues in Cyrenaica and Egypt
events.description: The Kitos War (or Second Jewish Revolt), which began in 115 CE, continues in 116 CE with intense fighting in Cyrenaica, Egypt, Cyprus, and Mesopotamia. Jewish communities rebel against Roman rule, and the revolt is characterized by violent clashes between Jewish rebels and Roman forces, as well as attacks on non-Jewish populations. The revolt is brutally suppressed by Roman forces under Lusius Quietus and other commanders. In 116 CE, the fighting is particularly intense, with massive casualties on both sides. The revolt results in the destruction of Jewish communities in the affected regions and the displacement of large populations.
key_figures: Jewish rebel leaders (names not consistently recorded)
events.certainty_note: The Kitos War is well-attested in multiple sources. The dates and extent of the revolt are accepted by modern scholarship, though the causes and significance are debated.

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu
events.description: The Han Dynasty continues military operations against the Xiongnu nomads on the northern frontier in 116 CE. Han forces conduct campaigns to secure the Silk Road trade routes and prevent Xiongnu raids into Chinese territory. These campaigns are costly in terms of resources and manpower, contributing to the financial strain on the Han treasury. The Xiongnu remain a persistent threat despite earlier Han victories. The campaigns are part of the broader Han strategy to maintain control of the western territories and the trade routes to Central Asia. Success is mixed, with tactical victories but no decisive strategic resolution.
key_figures: Han military commanders (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Hanshu (History of the Han Dynasty) and Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han)
Chinese historical records compiled by Sima Qian and later historians

events.certainty_note: Han military campaigns against the Xiongnu are well-attested in Chinese chronicles, but specific campaigns in 116 CE are often conflated with broader periods of conflict. Dating to this specific year is approximate.
cross_references: Part of Han-Xiongnu conflict (200 BCE – 50 CE and beyond)

events.title: Satavahana Empire at its height
events.description: The Satavahana Empire, under Gautamiputra Satakarni (r. c. 106–130 CE), is at the height of its power in 116 CE. The empire controls the Deccan Plateau and extends its influence to the coasts of the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal. Satavahana power is reflected in extensive coinage, urban development, and patronage of Buddhism and Brahminism. The empire's wealth derives from control of trade routes and agricultural production. Satavahana inscriptions and coins from this period attest to the empire's prosperity and cultural achievements. The empire is at its maximum territorial extent.
events.certainty_note: Satavahana territorial extent and prosperity are well-attested through numismatic, epigraphic, and archaeological evidence. The specific dating to 116 CE reflects the broader period of Satavahana dominance (c. 106–130 CE).
cross_references: Part of Indian Ocean trade network (1st–3rd century CE)

events.title: Kushan Empire consolidation and Silk Road control
events.description: The Kushan Empire, under Kanishka (if his reign extends to 116 CE) or his successors, consolidates its control of the Silk Road trade routes in 116 CE. The Kushans issue coins in multiple languages and scripts, reflecting their multicultural empire. Buddhist monasteries and stupas are constructed throughout the Kushan realm, and Buddhist texts are translated and disseminated. The Kushans maintain peaceful relations with neighboring empires, including the Satavahana Empire to the south and the Han Dynasty to the east. The Kushan Empire is at a period of territorial consolidation and cultural flourishing.
key_figures: Kanishka (Kushan Emperor, if reign extends to 116 CE) or his successor

sources.name: Numismatic evidence (Kushan coins with Buddhist imagery)
Archaeological evidence from Buddhist sites (Bamiyan, Gandhara)

events.certainty_note: Kushan cultural achievements are well-attested through numismatic and archaeological evidence. However, Kanishka's dates are disputed, and the specific events of 116 CE cannot be pinpointed.
cross_references: Part of Silk Road development (1st–3rd century CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The extent and sustainability of Trajan's conquest of Mesopotamia (event 116-001) is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that Trajan's control was tenuous and that the conquest was never fully consolidated. The rapid abandonment of these territories by Hadrian in 117 CE suggests that the conquest was indeed unsustainable. The Kitos War (event 116-002) is attested in sources, but the causes and extent of the revolt are debated. Some scholars question whether the revolt was primarily religious or whether economic and political grievances played a larger role. Kanishka's dates (event 116-005) remain highly disputed. No disconfirming evidence identified for events 116-003 and 116-004, though both reflect broad contextual trends rather than specific dated events.
historiographic_note: The year 116 CE is richly documented for the Roman Empire through coins, inscriptions, and later chronicles. Trajan's Parthian War and the Kitos War are among the most documented events of this period. However, contemporary written records are sparse; most narrative accounts derive from compilations written centuries later (Cassius Dio, Historia Augusta). Archaeological evidence (coins, inscriptions, architectural remains) provides more reliable information than narrative sources. For China, India, and Central Asia, documentation is moderate to sparse. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are essentially absent from the surviving written record for this year. The surviving sources are subject to court bias, legendary embellishment, and chronological compression. Roman sources tend to emphasize political and military events at the expense of economic and social history.

graph_edges.note: Trajan's conquest of Mesopotamia leads to Hadrian's withdrawal (117 CE)
Trajan's conquest is part of the broader Parthian War (114–117 CE)
The Kitos War continues and concludes in 117 CE
Han military campaigns against the Xiongnu are part of the broader Han-Xiongnu conflict
Satavahana prosperity continues until the empire's decline in the 2nd century CE
Kushan control of the Silk Road is integral to the development of the trade network
